chapterTitle: Chapter 0: How To Read This Book
title: How to use the system book
summary: A quick orientation to reading the Tutor architecture book as a practical map instead of a code dump.
transcript: Start here like you would open a map before a long drive. This book is not asking you to memorize every file. It is giving you a feel for the system: what the learner sees, where the tutor gets context, how local memory is saved, and which areas need extra care. Read it straight through when you are new. Come back chapter by chapter when you are debugging. And keep one distinction in your head as you go: this book explains the product architecture, while Graphify is still the code architecture map. The best rhythm is simple. First understand the learning experience. Then use Graphify when you need exact code paths, dependencies, and blast radius. This opening chapter is a map for reading the system book without getting buried in implementation details. In simple terms, the Tutor app has a foreground learning experience, a local memory layer, a server layer that talks to models and tools, and a maintenance layer that helps developers understand the code safely. Part 1: Read from the user outward. Start with what the learner sees: Study, Chat, Revision, Analytics, and Admin. Then move inward to the data that supports those screens. That order keeps the book practical because every technical part is tied to something visible in the product. Part 2: Separate live teaching from durable memory. A chat answer can adapt quickly, but a stored learning record needs a stronger trail. The book keeps returning to that distinction because it protects the learner from hidden memory changes and protects the builder from guessing what the system did. Part 3: Use the built-in books as operating manuals. Tutor System Architecture explains how the app is assembled. User Brain Architecture explains how learning state should become trustworthy. App Design Language explains how the interface should feel and behave. Together they are not marketing pages; they are local beta manuals. Part 4: Use audio as a guide, not as proof. The stored chapter audio is a friendly explanation of each chapter. It is checked in as a local MP3 so playback is fast, but it does not replace the source text, tests, or Admin evidence. If something matters, verify it in the app or the ledger. Part 5: Keep cloud work out of the first pass. This book is written for local beta. It explains local storage, local verification, local diagnostics, and local playback. AWS, tenant isolation, and production deployment can come later after the local loop is proven with real use. Part 6: Let Graphify steer code work. When changing the repository, agents should use Graphify before large file reads. That keeps architecture navigation focused and helps avoid accidental edits in unrelated areas. The practical takeaway is simple: the app is strongest when the reader, tutor, local memory, Admin, and Graphify each do one clear job. The learner should feel one coherent tutor, while the implementation keeps enough records for a developer to inspect what happened later. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what Chapter 0: How To Read This Book means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented.

title: The product shape
summary: A lively overview of Tutor as a document study workspace, AI tutor, memory system, and review surface.
transcript: The Product Map chapter is the wide shot. Tutor is not just a PDF viewer, and it is not just a chat box. It is a full study workspace, which means the important actions should feel connected: read something, ask a question, save the useful part, review it later, and inspect what the system did. Study is where the source material lives. Chat is where the tutor responds. The Library keeps learning material reusable. Revision turns ideas into recall. Admin gives the builder a clear window into model runs, tool calls, memory writes, and diagnostics. That is the shape the rest of the architecture protects. Every technical choice should make that loop calmer, clearer, and more trustworthy. The product map chapter explains the app as a learning workspace instead of a pile of features. The main idea is that the learner can read, ask, save, review, and inspect, all inside one local-first study loop. Part 1: Study is the entry point. Study is where a learner brings in a document, sees the reader, selects text, and starts asking grounded questions. It should feel like the desk where the learning session begins, not like a separate upload utility. Part 2: Chat is the live tutor. Chat turns the current document, selection, memory, and user message into a streamed tutoring response. The important product behavior is that the tutor should explain clearly while preserving enough context for later memory writes. Part 3: Revision is the reusable notebook. Revision is where temporary conversation turns become durable study material. It holds built-in architecture books, generated learning books, flashcards, and stored audio guides, so the learner can return later without replaying the whole chat. Part 4: Analytics summarizes learning progress. Analytics is not the tutor itself. It reads local records and turns them into progress signals: mastery, confidence, study activity, and review state. It helps the learner and builder see whether the system is producing useful learning traces. Part 5: Admin explains the machinery. Admin is the behind-the-scenes surface. It shows model runs, tool jobs, retrieval events, memory writes, voice events, artifact checks, corrections, and beta diagnostics. If the system makes a durable claim, Admin should help inspect it. Part 6: The map stays local first. The product can use model providers and voice services, but the current architecture should prove the local learning loop before moving responsibility to cloud infrastructure. That keeps beta testing concrete and debuggable. The practical takeaway is simple: the app is strongest when the reader, tutor, local memory, Admin, and Graphify each do one clear job. The learner should feel one coherent tutor, while the implementation keeps enough records for a developer to inspect what happened later. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what Chapter 1: Product Map means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented.

title: What the tools actually do
summary: A friendly translation of React, Vite, Dexie, Express, models, voice, search, PDF, and Graphify.
transcript: This chapter turns the tool list into plain language. React draws the interface. Vite keeps development fast. Zustand holds live UI state, like the current view, selected document, voice settings, and active book. Dexie stores the local learner notebook in the browser. Express handles the API routes that need a server boundary. Model providers answer, vision reads images or pages, search providers bring in current outside facts, and Graphify helps maintainers navigate the code architecture before they open a pile of files. The useful mental model is not a stack of impressive names. It is a relay team. Each tool owns a clear handoff, and the learner should feel the result as one steady study experience. This chapter translates the engineering stack into plain English. The goal is not to memorize library names. The goal is to understand which tool owns which part of the learner experience. Part 1: React builds the surfaces. React is the screen builder. It lets the app split Study, Chat, Revision, Admin, controls, panels, and cards into reusable pieces. When a learner clicks or changes state, React is what redraws the correct interface. Part 2: Zustand holds live UI state. Zustand is the small shared store for things like the active view, selected document, chat state, and settings. It is useful for live behavior, but it is not the durable learner brain by itself. Part 3: Dexie stores durable local records. Dexie wraps IndexedDB, the browser database. This is where local concepts, learning books, flashcards, evidence rows, model runs, tool jobs, retrieval events, corrections, and artifact records can survive beyond one screen refresh. Part 4: Express owns server routes. The Express server receives browser requests for chat, documents, search, trace explanations, voice, title generation, pricing, and debug activity. It is the bridge between the local app and external providers. Part 5: Provider SDKs do specific jobs. OpenRouter-compatible model calls produce tutor answers and learning-book updates. Deepgram handles voice and stored speech generation. Serper handles explicit live web search. Each provider should be visible through saved records when it matters. Part 6: Graphify helps maintainers navigate. Graphify is not the learner brain. It is the code architecture graph for agents and developers. It helps identify connected files before edits, which reduces broad repository reads and lowers the chance of touching the wrong surface. The practical takeaway is simple: the app is strongest when the reader, tutor, local memory, Admin, and Graphify each do one clear job. The learner should feel one coherent tutor, while the implementation keeps enough records for a developer to inspect what happened later. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what Chapter 2: Tools In Plain English means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented.

title: How the screen changes
summary: A short guide to App, navigation, lazy views, Zustand state, and context moving across screens.
transcript: Frontend Flow explains how the app changes shape without losing the learner. App reads the active view from Zustand, then renders Study, Analytics, Revision, Admin, or another focused surface. Heavy views load lazily, so the first workspace does not pay for everything at once. The store is the quiet handoff layer. It carries selected text, active document details, API key state, usage, voice settings, and active book context between screens. That is why a PDF quote can become a chat question, then a saved note, then a review card, without asking the learner to repeat themselves. The engineering goal is not clever navigation. It is continuity. The learner should feel like the app remembers the thread. The frontend flow chapter explains how a route becomes an experience. In simple terms, the app decides which main surface is active, renders that surface, and keeps motion and layout consistent around it. Part 1: App chooses the active screen. The top-level app reads the active view from the store and chooses Study, Analytics, Revision, or Admin. That means navigation is not just a link; it is a state change that controls which major learning surface is visible. Part 2: Study carries the reading workspace. Study has the document reader, file controls, and the live tutor panel nearby. It needs to keep the reader and chat connected because selected text, pages, and document state can shape the next answer. Part 3: Revision has a different mood. Revision uses a calmer paper-style reader because it is for review and saved knowledge. The same app can be dark and cinematic in Study, then book-like in Revision, as long as the route transition feels intentional. Part 4: Admin is dense on purpose. Admin is not meant to be a playful study surface. It is an operational dashboard, so it needs tabs, meters, filters, rows, and explicit state labels. Its job is to make hidden system activity inspectable. Part 5: Motion should clarify state. Animation is useful when it helps the learner understand a transition, a selected control, or a live AI state. It should not hide latency, cover errors, or make dense information harder to scan. Part 6: Responsive behavior is part of correctness. A book card, audio guide, PDF chip, and Admin table all need to fit on mobile and desktop. Browser QA matters because a build can pass while a real layout still clips or overflows. The practical takeaway is simple: the app is strongest when the reader, tutor, local memory, Admin, and Graphify each do one clear job. The learner should feel one coherent tutor, while the implementation keeps enough records for a developer to inspect what happened later. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what Chapter 3: Frontend Flow means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented. That is the standard for this book: simple language first, visible behavior second, and traceable local evidence third. The chapter guide should help a new reader understand the system before they need to inspect the implementation.

title: From document to study context
summary: A guide to uploads, PDF/image classification, extraction, and local document grounding.
transcript: Study and ingestion is where raw material becomes usable learning context. A learner uploads a PDF or image. The app works out what kind of document it is. Then extraction turns pages into text, images, and anchors the tutor can actually reference. That matters because grounding begins before the model speaks. If the learner asks about the current document, the local source should lead the answer. If the source is weak, missing, or ambiguous, the UI should make that visible instead of pretending. Ingestion is not just file handling. It is the first trust step before the tutor explains, quotes, summarizes, generates flashcards, or saves review material into the learner brain. Study and document ingestion are where source material enters the learning loop. The simple promise is this: when a learner uploads a paper or textbook, the app should preserve enough local context for useful tutoring and later review. Part 1: The reader creates visible context. A PDF or image is not just a file sitting in storage. It becomes pages, selections, highlights, and visible reading state. That visible state helps the tutor answer questions about what the learner is actually studying. Part 2: Extraction creates machine context. Document ingestion can classify a file, extract text, and prepare metadata that the tutor and memory layer can use. The extracted text is not magic; it is a local source that must be handled carefully and checked when used for durable claims. Part 3: Selection should matter. If the learner highlights a sentence or asks about a page, the tutor should prefer that local material before reaching for broad model knowledge or web search. That is the basic source-first behavior for a study app. Part 4: Multiple documents need clear UI. When more than one document is present, the app needs compact controls that show which file is active and let the learner switch or remove documents without losing the shape of the study session. Part 5: Ingestion failures need honest feedback. If extraction fails, the app should say what failed and keep the learner in a recoverable state. A failed extraction should not quietly create confident memory rows or pretend the source text exists. Part 6: Stored context feeds later memory. Good ingestion is important because later learning-book entries, source-span anchors, retrieval events, and citation checks all depend on having coherent local source context to reference. The practical takeaway is simple: the app is strongest when the reader, tutor, local memory, Admin, and Graphify each do one clear job. The learner should feel one coherent tutor, while the implementation keeps enough records for a developer to inspect what happened later. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what Chapter 4: Study And Document Ingestion means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented. That is the standard for this book: simple language first, visible behavior second, and traceable local evidence third. The chapter guide should help a new reader understand the system before they need to inspect the implementation.

title: The live tutor loop
summary: A focused explanation of streaming chat, source-first context, tool calls, web search, flashcards, and voice.
transcript: Chat is the live tutor loop. It gathers the learner message, selected text, local document context, settings, and sometimes memory context, then streams a response from the server. Tools can help, but they should not feel mysterious. A web search is for current outside facts. Vision is for pages or images. Flashcard generation creates review material. Source cards and learning notes need provenance. Voice adds immediacy, but still needs clear controls. The chapter's main lesson is control. Local-source questions should stay grounded locally. Tool results should be visible. Generated study objects should be useful right away, but reviewable before they become trusted learner-brain evidence. The tutor can feel fluid without becoming opaque. Chat and tutor tools are the live teaching loop. The learner sends a message, the app gathers context, the server streams an answer, tools may run, and the memory layer can save useful learning traces afterward. Part 1: The message is not alone. A good tutor request includes the user message plus the active document, selected text, current book, runtime settings, and relevant local memory. The model should not have to guess the learning situation from one sentence. Part 2: Streaming keeps the lesson alive. Server-sent events let the answer arrive piece by piece. That makes the tutor feel responsive, but the system still needs to record model starts, completions, failures, and fallbacks so Admin can inspect the request later. Part 3: Tools need visible boundaries. A tool call might search the web, generate flashcards, inspect context, or create a learning-book update. The learner may not need every detail in the foreground, but the system should save enough tool-job state for Admin. Part 4: Source-first behavior matters. If the question is about uploaded material, the tutor should use local source context first. Web search is for explicit web needs or current external facts, not a substitute for the document the learner is reading. Part 5: Memory updates happen after the turn. The foreground answer should stay fast. Durable memory can be written after the response, through a bounded path that records learning entries, concepts, artifacts, retrieval rows, and checks when available. Part 6: Voice should share the same path. Voice mode should not bypass chat architecture. Spoken user turns and assistant responses should be recorded and injected through the same learning context wherever possible, with Admin showing voice lifecycle and errors. The practical takeaway is simple: the app is strongest when the reader, tutor, local memory, Admin, and Graphify each do one clear job. The learner should feel one coherent tutor, while the implementation keeps enough records for a developer to inspect what happened later. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what Chapter 5: Chat And Tutor Tools means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented.

chapterTitle: Chapter 6: Memory, Dexie, And The Library
title: The local learner notebook
summary: A guide to Dexie tables, learning books, entries, evidence, artifacts, corrections, and why chat is not the memory.
transcript: Memory, Dexie, and the Library is the learner notebook chapter. A chat transcript can disappear, but useful learning state should survive as local records. Dexie stores books, entries, concepts, flashcards, evidence, model runs, tool jobs, retrieval events, artifacts, citations, and corrections. The MemoryOrchestrator is the bridge from a live conversation into durable study material. That bridge needs discipline. A generated note may be helpful. A flashcard may be ready to review. But evidence, mastery changes, and corrections need stronger rows with source, reason, and inspection paths. The Library is not a scrapbook of model output. It is the place where useful material becomes organized enough to revisit. Memory, Dexie, and the Library are where the app stops being only a chat box. The simple idea is that useful learning moments can become local records that the learner can inspect, review, correct, and reuse. Part 1: Dexie is local durable storage. Dexie saves records in the browser's IndexedDB. It is not AWS, not a remote tenant database, and not a model memory. It is the local notebook that makes beta learning flows testable without cloud infrastructure. Part 2: Learning books organize study output. A generated learning book can hold entries, concepts, risks, and notes from a session. This matters because a learner should not need to search through a chat transcript to find what they learned. Part 3: Evidence and mastery are stricter. The app can store many useful rows, but mastery should move only from validated learning evidence. A model summary can describe what happened; it should not silently increase a learner's score by itself. Part 4: Artifacts carry provenance. Generated notes, flashcards, source cards, and audio guides can all have artifact records and citation states. Those rows explain where the artifact came from and what local verifier, if any, has checked it. Part 5: Corrections are non-destructive. If something is wrong, the app should mark, supersede, or request review instead of erasing the history. That keeps the learner brain auditable and makes beta debugging possible. Part 6: The Library is the learner-facing result. Revision shows the stored books and review material. That is where the learner sees the benefit of local memory: saved explanations, concepts, flashcards, audio guides, and active recall. The practical takeaway is simple: the app is strongest when the reader, tutor, local memory, Admin, and Graphify each do one clear job. The learner should feel one coherent tutor, while the implementation keeps enough records for a developer to inspect what happened later. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what Chapter 6: Memory, Dexie, And The Library means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented. That is the standard for this book: simple language first, visible behavior second, and traceable local evidence third. The chapter guide should help a new reader understand the system before they need to inspect the implementation.

title: Review becomes reusable
summary: A guide to built-in books, generated learning books, flashcards, and stored chapter audio guides.
transcript: Revision is where learning stops being trapped in a chat transcript. Built-in books explain the system. Generated learning books save what the learner studied. Flashcards turn ideas into active recall. Chapter audio guides add a listening path, but they should not be live read-aloud every time someone presses play. They should be authored, stored, and calm enough to follow. The purpose is practical. Come back later, open the Library, and recover the topic through chapters, concepts, cards, and short explanations that sound like a guide, not a robot reading a manual. Revision is the slower half of the tutor. It gives the learner a place to return. Revision and active recall turn saved material into practice. The practical promise is that a learner can leave the live chat, come back later, and still have a useful study path. Part 1: Built-in books teach the product. The built-in books explain the app architecture, learner brain, and design system. They are part of local beta because they help the builder and learner understand what the app is supposed to do. Part 2: Generated books preserve sessions. When chat and document context produce useful notes, those notes can become generated learning-book entries. The learner gets a cleaner study artifact than a raw conversation transcript. Part 3: Flashcards create explicit checks. Active recall is different from passive reading. A flashcard answer can become review evidence when it is linked to a real concept and handled through the evidence-gated mastery path. Part 4: Audio guides support review. Stored audio guides let the learner listen to a chapter explanation without making a live TTS request. The audio should help comprehension, while the text and ledgers remain the source for verification. Part 5: Revision needs a calmer surface. The paper-style design is intentional. Review should feel like opening a careful notebook, not like staring at the live cosmic workspace. Different moods help the learner understand the mode they are in. Part 6: Corrections should flow back. If a learner or developer marks a record wrong, Revision and Admin should make that state visible. Review is safer when stale or disputed records are not treated like clean knowledge. The practical takeaway is simple: the app is strongest when the reader, tutor, local memory, Admin, and Graphify each do one clear job. The learner should feel one coherent tutor, while the implementation keeps enough records for a developer to inspect what happened later. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what Chapter 7: Revision And Active Recall means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented. That is the standard for this book: simple language first, visible behavior second, and traceable local evidence third. The chapter guide should help a new reader understand the system before they need to inspect the implementation.

title: Seeing the system work
summary: A guide to Analytics as learner progress and Admin as local beta observability.
transcript: Analytics and Admin answer different questions, and keeping them separate makes the app easier to trust. Analytics asks, how is the learner doing? It reads local progress, mastery movement, confidence, interaction patterns, and study activity. Admin asks, what did the system just do? It shows model runs, tool jobs, retrieval rows, memory events, evidence, artifacts, citations, corrections, tuning settings, and diagnostics. One surface is about learning progress. The other is about system behavior. Together they keep the app honest. A learner should not need Admin to study well, but the builder needs Admin to see when a model call failed, a tool overreached, or a memory write deserves review. Analytics and Admin are the app's inspection surfaces. They do not teach the lesson directly; they explain what the learning system recorded and how the local beta run is behaving. Part 1: Analytics summarizes learner progress. Analytics reads local records and turns them into progress signals: study activity, mastery, confidence, interactions, and review state. It should help the learner understand momentum without pretending every metric is perfect. Part 2: Admin follows one request at a time. Admin groups system events, model runs, and tool jobs by request id. That lets a developer ask what happened during this tutor turn instead of hunting through disconnected logs. Part 3: Model and tool rows make behavior legible. When a model starts, falls back, fails, or completes, Admin should show it. When a tool job runs, Admin should show its lifecycle. This turns hidden background behavior into reviewable state. Part 4: Memory and retrieval rows explain context. A learner-brain update or retrieval choice should leave a row with source, status, and summary. That does not prove every answer is correct, but it proves the system did something inspectable. Part 5: Source artifacts separate readiness from trust. An artifact can be ready to show while its citation state is not checked, unsupported, unavailable, or conflicting. Admin keeps those distinctions visible so local beta does not over-claim. Part 6: Diagnostics are local and capped. The beta export is meant for review, not cloud synchronization. It should be small, local-only, and explicit about what remains deferred until later production work. The practical takeaway is simple: the app is strongest when the reader, tutor, local memory, Admin, and Graphify each do one clear job. The learner should feel one coherent tutor, while the implementation keeps enough records for a developer to inspect what happened later. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what Chapter 8: Analytics And Admin means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented. That is the standard for this book: simple language first, visible behavior second, and traceable local evidence third. The chapter guide should help a new reader understand the system before they need to inspect the implementation.

title: One learner turn, end to end
summary: A story-style walkthrough of upload, ask, retrieve, answer, save, and inspect.
transcript: This chapter follows one learner turn from the inside. A document is uploaded. Study extracts context. The learner asks for help. Chat streams an explanation. A tool may retrieve a local passage, search the web for current facts, read an image, or generate an artifact. Memory may save a useful note, flashcard, citation, or evidence row. Admin records the model run, tool job, retrieval event, memory event, and any diagnostic signal worth inspecting. The example makes the background concrete. A strong tutor should explain the lesson. A trustworthy learning system should also be able to explain how that lesson was produced, which sources shaped it, and what changed afterward. The background workflow example follows one learner turn from upload to review. It is useful because architecture can sound abstract until you trace a real path through the app. Part 1: A document enters Study. The learner uploads a PDF or image, and Study prepares the visible reader plus any extracted local context. This starts the chain because the tutor now has source material to work with. Part 2: The learner asks a question. Chat gathers the message, selected page or text, active book context, runtime settings, and useful memory. The request should carry enough context for the model to answer the actual study problem. Part 3: The server streams the answer. The server chooses a model path, records provider behavior, streams chunks back, and captures failures or fallbacks. The foreground experience is the answer, but the backend event trail is also part of the product. Part 4: Tools and retrieval may run. The tutor may use document retrieval, web search when explicit, or helper logic for artifacts. Each background action should have a bounded purpose and a row that explains its status. Part 5: Memory writes after the lesson. When the turn completes, Memory can store the interaction, update a learning book, attach concepts, create artifact records, and run local provenance checks where supported. Part 6: Admin and Revision close the loop. Admin lets the builder inspect what happened, while Revision lets the learner review saved material. A complete local flow needs both: one for trust, one for learning. The practical takeaway is simple: the app is strongest when the reader, tutor, local memory, Admin, and Graphify each do one clear job. The learner should feel one coherent tutor, while the implementation keeps enough records for a developer to inspect what happened later. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what Chapter 9: A Background Workflow Example means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented. That is the standard for this book: simple language first, visible behavior second, and traceable local evidence third. The chapter guide should help a new reader understand the system before they need to inspect the implementation. For this phase, the voice guide is intentionally paced like a short walkthrough. It should be long enough to explain the idea, but still focused enough that a learner can listen before returning to the chapter text and the working app.

title: Which model does what
summary: A map of tutor chat, fallbacks, learning-book updates, vision, web search, voice, stored guides, and pricing.
transcript: The model map keeps provider behavior understandable. Chat usually goes through the configured text model path. Learning-book updates, trace explanations, vision, search summaries, and voice each have different jobs. That matters because a model call is never just magic. It has a purpose, a cost, a latency profile, and a failure mode. Web search is for current outside facts, not for replacing a learner's uploaded document. Live voice needs fast turns and graceful fallback. Stored chapter guides are different again: they are generated ahead of time, saved as local MP3s, and played back without another model call. The map helps the team ask a practical question every time: why is this provider involved right now? The model and provider map explains which outside services support which job. The simple rule is that provider calls should have a clear purpose, a visible boundary, and a local record when they shape the learning system. Part 1: Tutor chat uses a model provider. The live answer usually comes through OpenRouter-compatible chat models. The exact model can vary, but the app should record model starts, fallbacks, completions, costs, and errors when possible. Part 2: Learning-book updates are structured. A learning-book update is not just another chat answer. It asks a model to produce structured notes, concepts, and risks that can become durable local records. That makes provenance and validation more important. Part 3: Voice has separate jobs. Live voice can include listening, thinking, speaking, transcript turns, and interruptions. Stored chapter audio is different: it is generated ahead of time and played locally from checked-in assets. Part 4: Web search is explicit. Serper is for current external sources or explicit web-search requests. It should not replace local document context when the learner is asking about the uploaded source material. Part 5: Fallbacks need transparency. If one model fails and another model handles the request, Admin should make that visible. A fallback can save the session, but it should not become an invisible change in model behavior. Part 6: Local beta should not hide production gaps. Using providers locally does not mean the cloud architecture is done. The app can prove chat, voice, and stored audio locally while still deferring AWS hosting, tenant isolation, and production key policy. The practical takeaway is simple: the app is strongest when the reader, tutor, local memory, Admin, and Graphify each do one clear job. The learner should feel one coherent tutor, while the implementation keeps enough records for a developer to inspect what happened later. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what Chapter 10: Model And Provider Map means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented. That is the standard for this book: simple language first, visible behavior second, and traceable local evidence third. The chapter guide should help a new reader understand the system before they need to inspect the implementation.

title: Navigation and safety rails
summary: A guide to Graphify-first code navigation, debug surfaces, and safety boundaries.
transcript: Graphify, debugging, and safety is the maintainer's guardrail chapter. Graphify is the repository architecture graph. It is not the learner brain, and that distinction really matters. Agents should use Graphify to route changes before reading half the repository. Debugging surfaces show what the app actually recorded. Safety boundaries describe what should never happen silently: hidden model internals, ungrounded mastery changes, destructive corrections, confusing generated artifacts, or cloud assumptions before beta testing. The theme is disciplined visibility. Move quickly when the code path is small. Slow down when shared state, server contracts, or learner memory are involved. Either way, leave a trail the team can inspect. Graphify, debugging, and safety are about how maintainers change the app without losing the map. The learner brain and the code graph are different things, and this chapter keeps that boundary clear. Part 1: Graphify is the code architecture graph. Graphify maps files, symbols, imports, calls, and communities in the repository. It helps agents find connected code before editing. It is not the user-facing learner concept graph. Part 2: Graph-first reads reduce confusion. Large repository scans can waste time and pull unrelated files into the decision. Graphify queries and paths give a smaller starting point, then the agent can read only directly connected files. Part 3: Debugging should follow evidence. A console warning, failed route, or broken UI state is a symptom. The safer workflow is to map the connected files, inspect the live behavior, then patch the narrow cause rather than guessing. Part 4: Generated graph artifacts are controlled. The `graphify-out` files should not be hand-edited. They are regenerated when the task explicitly includes graph maintenance or code architecture changes, not after every ordinary message. Part 5: Safety means preserving user work. The worktree may contain edits from the user or other agents. A good maintainer reads status, stages only the intended files, and never reverts unrelated changes just to make the tree look clean. Part 6: Verification is part of the change. Lint, build, focused tests, browser QA, and Graphify regeneration are not ceremony. They are how the team proves that an implementation slice still matches the architecture book. The practical takeaway is simple: the app is strongest when the reader, tutor, local memory, Admin, and Graphify each do one clear job. The learner should feel one coherent tutor, while the implementation keeps enough records for a developer to inspect what happened later. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what Chapter 11: Graphify, Debugging, And Safety means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented. That is the standard for this book: simple language first, visible behavior second, and traceable local evidence third. The chapter guide should help a new reader understand the system before they need to inspect the implementation.

title: Where to be careful
summary: A short guide to high-risk files, local verification, browser QA, and preserving product boundaries.
transcript: Maintenance Boundaries tells you where to slow down. Content books and copy are usually easy to change. Dexie schema, server routes, SSE shapes, websocket paths, Zustand fields, chat streaming, document ingestion, and generated Graphify artifacts are more sensitive. The chapter is not trying to make the project feel fragile. It is teaching proportional verification. Small copy change? Keep the check focused. Shared state or API behavior? Run broader tests. Visual change? Open the real app in the browser. Memory or evidence behavior? Add a regression test and inspect Admin. Good maintenance is not ceremony. It is attention with evidence, especially around the places where a quiet bug could mislead the learner. Maintenance boundaries explain where to be careful. The simple message is that not every file carries the same risk, and a local beta app becomes safer when changes stay scoped to the right surface. Part 1: Some files are content surfaces. Book text, chapter audio scripts, and some UI copy can be changed with relatively low risk, as long as tests and browser checks still pass. These edits should still respect the product truth. Part 2: Some files are contract surfaces. Server routes, SSE event shapes, WebSocket paths, Dexie schemas, and Zustand state fields affect many parts of the app. They need extra graph context and broader verification. Part 3: Generated assets need regeneration, not manual edits. Graphify artifacts and generated audio files should come from their tools. Manual editing can make them inconsistent with the source that future agents rely on. Part 4: Tests should match the risk. A small text change may need formatting and build checks. A memory schema change needs focused tests, migration awareness, and runtime QA. Verification should scale with the blast radius. Part 5: Dirty worktrees are normal. A developer or another agent may have untracked or modified files. The right move is to preserve unrelated work, understand any overlapping file, and stage only the intended implementation slice. Part 6: The architecture book is a living contract. When implementation changes, the book should move with it. If the book says a feature is verified, the source and tests need to prove that claim at the same scope. The practical takeaway is simple: the app is strongest when the reader, tutor, local memory, Admin, and Graphify each do one clear job. The learner should feel one coherent tutor, while the implementation keeps enough records for a developer to inspect what happened later. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what Chapter 12: Maintenance Boundaries means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented. That is the standard for this book: simple language first, visible behavior second, and traceable local evidence third. The chapter guide should help a new reader understand the system before they need to inspect the implementation.

title: Quick tour of the learner brain
summary: A lively guide to the foreground tutor, local learner-brain ledger, and background worker contract.
transcript: Let us zoom out and make the whole architecture feel simple. LearningAI is not meant to feel like eight separate tools taped together. It should feel like one attentive tutor sitting with the learner. The learner reads, asks questions, listens, reviews, and comes back later. That is the foreground experience. It should be smooth, quick, and human. Behind that calm surface, though, the app is doing a lot of careful bookkeeping. It is saving learning books, concept records, evidence events, model runs, tool jobs, retrieval traces, citations, correction requests, and generated artifacts. The point of this chapter is to separate those two speeds. The visible tutor can move fast. If the learner sounds confused, the tutor can change examples immediately. If the learner asks by voice, the tutor should respond naturally and stop when interrupted. If a document page is visible, the tutor should use that local context before wandering onto the web. But durable learner memory should move more slowly. A permanent record should not be created just because a model had a hunch. It should have a source, a reason, and a visible state. Think of the system as three layers. First, the foreground tutor handles the real-time lesson. Second, the learner brain stores local state and trust records. Third, background workers do bounded jobs like retrieval, artifact creation, summarization, and verification. That division keeps the product powerful without making it slippery. The learner gets a responsive teacher. The builder gets an audit trail. Admin can show what happened behind the scenes. Revision can turn a session into study material. Graphify can help agents navigate the code architecture without confusing that map with the learner brain. The big promise is this: LearningAI can adapt in the moment, but it should only change durable learner state when there is evidence. That is the shape to keep in mind as you read the rest of the book. One useful way to picture this is to imagine a classroom with a teacher, a notebook, and a lab assistant. The teacher is the foreground tutor. It is the part the learner talks to. The notebook is the learner brain. It keeps the record of what was studied, what was generated, what was reviewed, and what evidence exists. The lab assistant is the background worker layer. It can fetch context, run a tool, prepare a study artifact, or check a local record, but it should not quietly rewrite the notebook without a trace. That picture makes the product easier to reason about. Voice mode and chat mode are both ways to talk to the teacher. Revision is where the notebook becomes useful later. Admin is where the builder looks behind the curtain. Graphify is the map of the code that helps agents avoid wandering through the repository. These pieces should cooperate, not compete. If a learner asks a question in voice, that turn should still be stored and routed like a real tutor turn. If a tool runs in the background, Admin should show it. If memory changes, the ledger should explain why.

title: The ledger is the trust layer
summary: A guide to durable learner state, typed records, sources, and correction paths.
transcript: This chapter is about trust. The learner brain is not a mysterious memory cloud. In the local beta, it is a set of typed records in the browser. That sounds less glamorous, but it is exactly what makes the system understandable. Every durable record should answer a few plain questions. What changed? Why did it change? What source, learner action, or system event supports it? And if it is wrong, how can someone inspect it or correct it? That is why the architecture keeps a ledger instead of letting everything blur into one conversation history. A generated note is not the same thing as verified source evidence. A flashcard is not automatically proof that the answer is correct. A model run is not a mastery signal by itself. A source card, citation state, artifact record, correction event, and mastery delta each mean something specific. The ledger gives those meanings a home. For example, when the tutor generates learning notes, the app can save local provenance: the entry id, the book or conversation anchor, whether external content was fetched, and now source-span preview anchors when document text exists. That proves local traceability. It does not prove every sentence is true. That distinction is the whole spirit of the ledger. Useful things can exist before they are fully verified, but their status must stay visible. Admin should be able to show which rows are verified, not checked, unavailable, conflicting, or unsupported. Corrections should be non-destructive during beta, so the system can mark records stale, superseded, or disputed without silently erasing history. If you remember one thing from this chapter, remember this: the ledger is where a warm tutor becomes accountable software. It lets the app improve the learning experience while still leaving enough evidence for a human to ask, why did the system believe this, and can I trust it? In practice, this ledger-first design keeps the app from turning into a black box. Suppose the tutor creates a flashcard. The flashcard can be stored, reviewed, and shown in Revision, but it should also keep enough provenance for Admin to inspect the batch, the card ids, and the local anchors that produced it. Suppose the learner marks something wrong. The correction should not vanish into a vague preference. It should become a correction event with a target, an action, a reason, and downstream effects. Suppose an audio guide is stored. The app can verify that the guide points to a checked-in MP3, has a transcript, belongs to the right book and chapter, and did not fetch external content during the check. Each row has a narrow meaning. That narrowness is a strength. It lets the system say yes, this part is traceable, while also saying no, this does not prove the entire generated explanation is factually perfect. The learner brain becomes trustworthy because it can speak in these smaller, precise claims.

title: Fast teaching, careful memory
summary: A walkthrough of live tutoring adaptation versus evidence-gated memory updates.
transcript: The teaching loop is the part of the architecture that should feel alive. When the learner asks a question, the tutor should not behave like a static textbook. It should notice the context, the current document, the active book, previous confusion, and the learner's immediate wording. It can choose a shorter explanation, ask an active recall question, use a concrete analogy, slow down, or switch examples. In voice mode, it should also respect timing. Short turns matter. Interruptions matter. If the learner starts speaking, the app should stop playback and listen. That responsiveness is not a bonus feature. It is what makes the tutor feel like a tutor. But the chapter also draws a hard line. Fast adaptation is not the same as durable memory. The system can use soft signals to decide what to say next, but changing long-term learner state needs evidence. If mastery goes up, there should be a real evidence event, such as a flashcard review, a recall check, or another source-linked action. If confidence changes, the system should record what influenced it. If a generated learning note is saved, it should have provenance and local anchors. The app should not quietly promote a model summary into proof. So the teaching loop has two rhythms. The foreground lesson moves quickly and gracefully. The background learner brain writes carefully and visibly. Chat mode and voice mode both need to feed the same memory pathway. A spoken turn should not disappear just because it came through audio. It should become transcript turns, model activity, memory events, and learning-book updates when appropriate. The goal is a coherent flow: user asks, tutor responds, tools run when needed, memory records what actually happened, and Admin can explain the run afterward. If the loop works, the learner feels momentum, and the builder still has an audit trail. A concrete flow makes the distinction easier. The learner asks, explain this section. The tutor looks at the active document, the selected text, the current book, and recent memory. It answers in a way that fits the moment. Maybe it asks a follow-up question. Maybe it suggests a flashcard. Maybe in voice mode it keeps the answer short enough to follow. Those are fast choices. After the turn, the background path can decide what should be saved. A learning entry may be written. A generated note artifact may be recorded. Source-span preview anchors may be attached if document text was available. A retrieval event or memory event may be logged. If the learner later reviews a flashcard and answers correctly, that explicit review can become evidence for mastery. The key is that every durable movement has a trace. The tutor can be conversational without becoming careless, and the memory system can be careful without making the live lesson feel slow. That is why this chapter keeps returning to one question: what should happen immediately, and what should be saved permanently? If the team answers that question carefully, chat and voice can feel fluid without turning memory into guesswork.

title: Grounding is the main boundary
summary: An energetic guide to local-source-first retrieval, generated artifacts, and visible citation states.
transcript: Retrieval is where the tutor decides what material deserves attention before answering. The rule is simple, but very important: source material close to the learner should usually come first. If the learner is asking about the current PDF page, selected text, an uploaded document, a generated learning book, or a saved chapter, the app should use that local context before reaching outward. Web search is still useful, but it has a narrower job. Use it for current outside facts, explicit external research, or questions where freshness matters. Do not use web search as a lazy replacement for the learner's own document. Citations and artifacts are the visible side of that same boundary. A source card should say where it came from. A citation should have a state. A generated artifact should keep provenance. A flashcard, chart, note, code snippet, or audio guide can be helpful while still being marked not checked or locally verified only for a specific claim. This is where the architecture refuses to overclaim. A locally verified source-card link does not prove the web page content is true. A generated-note provenance check proves ledger coherence, not sentence-level truth. A stored audio guide manifest check proves the local MP3 and transcript metadata are connected, not that the audio content has been independently transcribed. Those boundaries protect the learner. They also protect the builder from believing the system is more mature than it is. The ideal experience is not heavy or suspicious. It is calm and clear. The learner should see useful study material. Admin should reveal the trust state when needed. Corrections should provide a path to challenge or supersede questionable records. Retrieval, artifacts, and citations are not paperwork. They are how LearningAI keeps powerful assistance connected to inspectable evidence. This also explains why tool calling needs clarity. A tool call is not just a hidden implementation detail. If the tutor used a document page, searched the web, generated flashcards, or asked a model to summarize a learning book, the system should be able to show that path. The learner may not need to see every low-level event during the lesson, but Admin should be able to inspect it later. That matters when a result is surprising. Did the answer come from the uploaded source, from web search, from a memory summary, or from the model's own reasoning? Did the artifact get verified locally, or is it still not checked? Did a citation conflict with a saved source field? These questions are not about slowing the learner down. They are about making the background layer legible. A good interface can keep the foreground calm while still preserving the evidence trail underneath. The practical test is simple. When a learner asks where an answer came from, the app should have something better than trust me. It should be able to point to the source path, the artifact state, and the local check that was actually performed.

title: Admin is the operating room
summary: A clear guide to model, tool, memory, retrieval, evidence, artifact, correction, and tuning surfaces.
transcript: Admin is the operating room for the local beta. It is not meant to be the learner's main study surface. The learner should be able to read, chat, listen, and revise without staring at diagnostics. But the builder needs a place to see what the system actually did. That is Admin's job. It should answer questions like these: which model ran, which provider was used, how long did the run take, what settings shaped the answer, which tools were called, what retrieval rows were created, what memory events were written, and which artifacts or citation states changed? If something failed, Admin should show the failure plainly. If a tool was skipped because of budget or source policy, that should be visible too. Runtime tuning belongs next to observability because tuning without evidence is just guessing. The app needs controls for local-source preference, web-search behavior, tool budgets, memory context, voice settings, diagnostics, and verifier coverage. But those controls should be humble. A meter should say what it measures. A local verifier should say what it checked and what it did not check. A green state should not imply a broader truth than the verifier actually proved. This matters especially for the learner brain. If memory updates, retrieval, citations, and mastery movement are hidden, the system becomes hard to trust and harder to improve. Admin turns hidden background behavior into something inspectable. It also creates a practical feedback loop for development. You can change a prompt, tune a setting, or fix a tool path, then watch the ledgers and meters to see whether the run became clearer. That is the operating-room metaphor: not dramatic, not decorative, just a clean place to see the system breathe. The tuning side should feel practical, not mystical. If the user changes a setting, the system should make clear what that setting can affect. A source-first setting can influence whether local document context is preferred. A tool budget can constrain how much background work happens in one turn. Voice settings can affect live agent behavior, but they do not change the stored audio guide assets that are already checked in. Diagnostics can export local beta state, but they do not create production cloud safety by themselves. Admin is strongest when it ties every control to observable evidence. Change a setting, run a lesson, then inspect model runs, tool jobs, retrieval rows, memory rows, artifact states, and voice events. That loop is how the product becomes tunable. Without Admin, the team would be guessing. With Admin, the system can show what it recorded, what it skipped, and where the next implementation slice should focus. That makes Admin less like a secret developer console and more like a tuning dashboard. It gives the builder enough evidence to improve the system without asking the learner to carry the debugging burden. For a beta tester, that means a very practical routine: try one lesson, open Admin, and check whether the meters match what really happened. If the learner spoke, there should be a voice record. If a tool ran, there should be a tool record. If memory changed, there should be a memory record. The dashboard earns trust by matching the session.

title: Voice should respect timing
summary: A natural explanation of stored chapter guides, live voice boundaries, and playback controls.
transcript: Voice and audio are both about timing, but they serve different moments. Live voice mode is for conversation. It needs to start quickly, listen well, speak in short useful turns, and stop when the learner interrupts. If voice mode keeps talking over the learner, it stops feeling like a tutor. If it forgets the conversation afterward, it stops being part of the learner brain. So voice turns need to become real transcript turns, learning-book updates when appropriate, model and voice events, and Admin-visible activity. Stored chapter audio is a different pattern. A chapter overview should not be a live text-to-speech reading of the page every time someone presses play. It should be an authored guide. Write the explanation first, make it sound like a clear teacher, generate the MP3 once, store it locally, and play it with familiar controls. The user should get play, pause, progress, seeking, and speed choices like one times and one point five times. The UI should not show internal metadata like generated script labels or storage timestamps. It should feel like a simple study card. The script should also breathe. A good overview explains the chapter in plain language, gives examples, repeats the main distinction, and leaves natural space for the idea to land. For this book, that means explaining the learner brain rather than reading every paragraph. Live voice helps during active tutoring. Stored audio helps during review. Both should feed the same product philosophy: be useful, be inspectable, and do not pretend to be more than you are. Voice mode can be lively. Stored guides can be warm and steady. Together they give the learner another path through the material without breaking the trust boundaries of the architecture. The important product move is to connect voice to the same architecture as chat. Voice should not be a shiny side feature that bypasses memory. When a learner speaks, the system should capture the user's turn, the assistant's spoken response, the session timing, and the relevant voice-agent events. If the conversation produces useful learning material, it should enter the same learning-book and artifact pathways as typed chat. If the live voice backend is unavailable, the UI should say so plainly and fall back to text or stored audio where appropriate. Stored chapter audio has a different reliability profile. It is not waiting on a live voice websocket. The MP3 is in the public audio folder, the manifest points to it, and the reader can show both custom controls and native browser controls. That makes review dependable. Live voice is for dialogue. Stored audio is for guided study. Both are useful, but they should never be confused. That separation keeps the experience honest. A learner should know when they are listening to a prepared chapter guide, when they are talking to a live voice tutor, and when the app had to fall back to text. Each mode can still feed the same learner brain, but the interface should not blur what happened.

title: Prove it locally before cloud
summary: A roadmap guide for proving learner-brain behavior before AWS or production sync work.
transcript: The local beta roadmap is deliberately grounded. It says: prove the learner brain locally before building cloud architecture around it. That does not mean cloud sync, tenant isolation, backups, production dashboards, or AWS are unimportant. They are important later. But infrastructure should not arrive before the product can explain itself in the browser. First, the local flow needs to work end to end. Chat mode and voice mode should both create usable tutor turns. Tool calls should be recorded clearly. Retrieval should prefer local source material when the question is about the learner's document or book. Memory should write learning entries, concepts, evidence, artifacts, and citation states with visible boundaries. Flashcard reviews should update mastery only when a real concept and explicit evidence exist. Generated notes should be traceable. Audio guides should be stored assets with manifest integrity. Admin should show model runs, tool jobs, voice events, memory events, retrieval rows, corrections, diagnostics, and tuning settings. That is already a serious system. The roadmap is saying: get that right before scaling it. If local memory cannot explain why a record changed, a cloud database will not make it trustworthy. If a correction path is confusing in one browser, multi-user sync will make the confusion harder. If voice turns do not join the same memory pathway as text turns, production deployment will only preserve the split. So this chapter is not anti-cloud. It is pro-sequencing. Earn trust locally, test the actual flows, use Admin to inspect them, and only then decide which AWS pieces deserve to exist. A local-first roadmap also gives the team better tests. Before cloud work, the app can prove concrete flows. Upload a document, ask through chat, ask through voice, generate a learning entry, inspect Admin, review the saved book, play the stored audio guide, answer a flashcard, and confirm the evidence ledger changed for the right reason. That is a real beta story. It is much stronger than saying the architecture is ready because the diagrams look good. The current priority is to make these loops work together in the product. Chat mode, voice mode, tool calls, retrieval, memory writes, Admin observability, Revision review, and stored audio should form one coherent system. Cloud sync can come later. First the local app needs to prove it can teach, remember, explain itself, and recover from mistakes. This roadmap also protects the learner. It says the app should become trustworthy in small observable steps, not through a giant hidden launch where memory, voice, tools, and cloud sync all change at once. The first beta milestone is therefore not a cloud launch. It is a complete local loop that a real learner can repeat: learn, ask, speak, save, review, inspect, and correct. Once that loop is steady, the next phase can decide which cloud pieces are worth adding.

title: Shared words keep the system honest
summary: A closing guide to precise vocabulary, trusted references, and source boundaries.
transcript: The sources and glossary chapter may look like the quiet ending of the book, but it is doing important work. A system like LearningAI can become confusing very quickly if the words get loose. Evidence, mastery, confidence, citation, artifact, correction, retrieval, memory, and learner brain all need precise meanings. Evidence is not just a model saying the learner understands. Evidence is a recorded learner action, review event, source-linked event, or another explicit signal that the system can point to. A mastery delta is a stored movement from one state to another, not a vibe. A correction is a visible review action, not a silent rewrite. A citation has a state. An artifact has provenance. A generated note can be useful without being fully source-span verified. A stored audio guide can be checked for manifest integrity without claiming the audio was independently fact-checked. A learner brain is the local ledger and orchestration around learning state. It is not Graphify, because Graphify maps the code architecture. It is not a secret cloud memory either, because cloud work is deferred until beta proves the local flow. The sources matter for the same reason. Product ideas can be inspired by research, provider docs, security patterns, and observability practice, but the local app still has to say what it actually implements today. Precise words keep the book honest. They also help future agents make safer changes. When someone reads this glossary, they should understand the promises the app is allowed to make, the promises it is not allowed to make yet, and the evidence needed to move from one state to the next. This vocabulary also helps with future implementation decisions. If someone says the learner brain is complete, ask which evidence proves that. Does chat mode store the right records? Does voice mode feed the same memory pathway? Are tool calls visible? Are model runs and retrieval events inspectable? Are generated artifacts labeled with the right trust state? Are corrections non-destructive? Do stored audio guides play from local assets rather than live read-aloud calls? These questions turn abstract confidence into concrete checks. The glossary keeps the team from drifting into impressive but vague language. It says: use ordinary words, make the boundaries visible, and do not claim more than the system can prove today. That is how LearningAI can keep becoming more capable without losing the user's trust. Use the glossary as a final checklist. If a feature cannot say what evidence it uses, what state it writes, what status it exposes, and how it can be corrected, then it is not ready to be treated as part of the durable learner brain. This is also the simplest way to explain the architecture to a non-technical reader. The app should remember useful learning moments, show why it remembers them, and let the user correct the record. Everything else is an implementation detail that has to serve those three promises.

title: How the surfaces connect
summary: A guide to the reader's wireframe map and the relationship between Study, Chat, Library, Admin, and background systems.
transcript: Wireframe Connections is the design map. It shows how the learner moves through Study, Chat, Library, Admin, and the background systems that support them. The point is not decoration. It is orientation. A good interface should make the main routes feel obvious: read here, ask there, save this, review later, inspect system behavior when needed. Advanced surfaces like Admin and Graphify can exist, but they should not be confused with the learner brain. Admin observes the local app. Graphify maps the code architecture. The learner brain is the study ledger. This chapter keeps those surfaces visible and separate, so the product can feel powerful without feeling tangled. The wireframe connections chapter explains how the product surfaces relate to each other. In simple terms, the learner moves between reading, tutoring, reviewing, analytics, and Admin without those surfaces becoming separate products. Part 1: Study and Chat sit close together. The reader and tutor belong near each other because questions usually come from the visible document. The design should make it easy to select, ask, and return to the source without losing context. Part 2: Revision is a calmer destination. Revision is where saved material becomes a book, a card, or an audio guide. It should feel less like a live control room and more like a place to reread and practice. Part 3: Admin is connected but separate. Admin needs to inspect the same events that Study, Chat, voice, and memory create, but it should not crowd the learner's main workspace. It is for tuning and diagnosis. Part 4: Analytics summarizes rather than controls. Analytics should show progress and usage patterns without becoming the place where learning happens. It is a window into accumulated state. Part 5: The graph is a product map, not decoration. Wireframe links should help explain how a turn moves through the system. A connection is useful when it tells the builder what state, artifact, or action crosses a boundary. Part 6: Mobile flow still matters. On small screens, the surfaces need to collapse without hiding the learner's next obvious action. A readable mobile wireframe is part of the design contract. The practical takeaway is that visual design is part of the learning system, not decoration. The interface should help the learner understand where they are, what changed, what can be reviewed, and which controls affect the next local beta run. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what Wireframe Connections means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented. That is the standard for this book: simple language first, visible behavior second, and traceable local evidence third. The chapter guide should help a new reader understand the system before they need to inspect the implementation. For this phase, the voice guide is intentionally paced like a short walkthrough. It should be long enough to explain the idea, but still focused enough that a learner can listen before returning to the chapter text and the working app.

title: Two moods, one product
summary: A guide to Cosmic Obsidian for active study and paper-style calm for Revision and documentation.
transcript: Theme System explains the app's two-mood design. Study, Chat, and core AI surfaces use Cosmic Obsidian: very dark surfaces, neon accents, glass, and motion. That is the active workspace, where the tutor feels alive and responsive. Revision and documentation become quieter, with paper color, serif type, and notebook texture. That is the return path, where the learner needs calm enough to read, remember, and review. The contrast is intentional. The app can feel futuristic without making every screen shout. The design system protects both energies: focused intensity for active learning, and analog steadiness for reflection. Good style here is not decoration. It is pacing. The theme system chapter explains why LearningAI has two strong visual moods. The live learning workspace can be cosmic, dark, and energetic, while revision and notes can feel like paper and careful study. Part 1: Cosmic Obsidian is for focus and AI state. The dark surface, neon accents, glass, and motion help the Study and Chat areas feel premium and alive. They should highlight meaningful AI state, not cover every inch of the interface. Part 2: Paper mode is for review. Revision uses warmer paper backgrounds, serif type, and quieter spacing because review asks for a different kind of attention. It should feel like opening a notebook, not like staying in the live tutor cockpit. Part 3: Color should mark meaning. Violet, blue, and orange accents should help the learner read state: active AI surfaces, selected controls, warnings, and important actions. Too much accent color makes everything feel equally urgent. Part 4: Glass should belong to assistant surfaces. Liquid glass effects are strongest when they signal AI assistance, overlays, or meaningful control groups. They should not become random decoration on every panel. Part 5: Motion should have a reason. Spring motion and transitions should clarify changes: route switches, active tabs, opening panels, play controls, and assistant status. Motion that hides information or causes layout shifts works against learning. Part 6: The design system must stay testable. A beautiful surface still has to pass browser QA. Text must fit, controls must be reachable, audio cards must not overflow, and Admin tables must remain scannable. The practical takeaway is that visual design is part of the learning system, not decoration. The interface should help the learner understand where they are, what changed, what can be reviewed, and which controls affect the next local beta run. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what Theme System means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented. That is the standard for this book: simple language first, visible behavior second, and traceable local evidence third. The chapter guide should help a new reader understand the system before they need to inspect the implementation. For this phase, the voice guide is intentionally paced like a short walkthrough. It should be long enough to explain the idea, but still focused enough that a learner can listen before returning to the chapter text and the working app.

title: Small components, real behavior
summary: A guide to rendered previews that make buttons, cards, panels, and controls easier to reason about.
transcript: UI Component Snapshots turn design rules into visible examples. Instead of describing every component in abstract language, this chapter shows compact rendered pieces: cards, controls, panels, charts, meters, and interaction states. The value is practical. When the app grows, snapshots help keep style decisions consistent. They also make regressions easier to notice, because a component that looked calm, readable, and aligned yesterday should still feel that way after today's code change. This is especially helpful in a product with two moods. A glassy AI control and a paper-style revision card can belong to the same app, as long as their behavior, spacing, and hierarchy feel deliberate. UI component snapshots are small examples of how the design system behaves. They matter because reusable components carry product decisions: density, motion, trust state, feedback, and reviewability.
