Part 1: Cards should frame repeated objects. A card is useful for a book, source artifact, metric, flashcard, or gallery snapshot. It should not turn every section of the page into a floating object, because that weakens hierarchy. Part 2: Controls should be familiar. Play, pause, speed, seek, tabs, toggles, sliders, and remove buttons should behave like users expect. Familiar controls make advanced AI features feel less mysterious. Part 3: Status labels need plain words. Citation states, tool status, model state, and correction state should use labels that explain what happened. A learner or builder should not need to decode internal implementation names. Part 4: Empty and error states count. If a table has no rows, a route is unavailable, or media playback fails, the UI should show a real state. Silence makes the product feel broken and makes Admin less useful. Part 5: Snapshots should reflect real behavior. A design snapshot is most useful when it matches real components: the same chip rail, audio card, verifier meter, or timeline style the app actually uses. Part 6: Mobile should not be an afterthought. The same component may become a horizontal chip list, a stacked card, or a compact tab row on mobile. The design system needs responsive constraints, not one desktop mockup. The practical takeaway is that visual design is part of the learning system, not decoration. The interface should help the learner understand where they are, what changed, what can be reviewed, and which controls affect the next local beta run. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what UI Component Snapshots means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented. That is the standard for this book: simple language first, visible behavior second, and traceable local evidence third. The chapter guide should help a new reader understand the system before they need to inspect the implementation. For this phase, the voice guide is intentionally paced like a short walkthrough. It should be long enough to explain the idea, but still focused enough that a learner can listen before returning to the chapter text and the working app.

title: Controls that explain the system
summary: A guide to local beta controls for tuning, diagnostics, source checks, correction overlays, and stored audio guides.
transcript: Local Beta Control Patterns is about trust through controls. Runtime settings, diagnostics exports, source checks, correction overlays, artifact provenance, citation states, local verifiers, and stored audio guides all need clear UI. The user should see what happened, what can be tuned, and what is still only local beta. That status matters. A button that runs a local check should say what it checked. A meter should reveal its source. A stored audio guide should play like normal media, without pretending to be live intelligence. This chapter is the product design version of the learner-brain rule: do useful things, but make the status visible. Control without clarity is just another hidden system. Local beta control patterns are the design bridge between beautiful learning UI and inspectable system behavior. They show the user or builder what changed, why it changed, and what can be tuned next. Part 1: PDF chips keep document switching compact. When multiple documents exist, the selected PDF should be visible and easy to change. A compact chip rail gives the learner control without taking over the reading surface. Part 2: Request timelines turn logs into stories. Admin should group model runs, tool jobs, retrieval events, and server events by request. That lets one tutor turn be reviewed as a story instead of a scattered pile of rows. Part 3: Voice timelines make live speech debuggable. Voice mode needs to show session start, Deepgram settings, speaking and listening transitions, transcript turns, interruptions, and errors. The UI can feel magical only if the diagnostics stay grounded. Part 4: Correction overlays protect beta trust. Mark-wrong, deletion-review, and supersede controls should not erase history. They should add visible correction state so questionable records stop pretending to be clean. Part 5: Citation checks need separate states. A generated artifact can be ready but not verified, verified only for local provenance, unsupported, unavailable, or conflicting. Good controls keep those states separate. Part 6: Audio generation controls should be boring. The audio generator has one practical job: verify the plan, produce stored MP3s, and let the reader play them locally. The playback UI should be reliable, clear, and easy to inspect. The practical takeaway is that visual design is part of the learning system, not decoration. The interface should help the learner understand where they are, what changed, what can be reviewed, and which controls affect the next local beta run. This chapter should be understandable when heard without looking at the code. The listener should come away knowing what Local Beta Control Patterns means, where it appears in the app, what local evidence can prove it, and which future production claims still need more work after beta. A useful beta check is to open the actual screen, perform the action described in the chapter, and then inspect the saved local state. If the UI, Admin rows, audio guide, and source text all agree, the architecture is becoming real rather than just documented. That is the standard for this book: simple language first, visible behavior second, and traceable local evidence third. The chapter guide should help a new reader understand the system before they need to inspect the implementation.
